Article Title: 'Not talking about some 56-year-old youth': PM Modi takes apparent swipe at Rahul Gandhi
Article Text:
PM Modi takes apparent swipe at Rahul Gandhi

NEW DELHI: Prime Minister Narendra Modi on Monday took a veiled swipe at leader of opposition Rahul Gandhi while praising India's young innovators saying he was "referring to the 28-year-old not 56".Addressing reporters ahead of the commencement of the Monsoon Session of Parliament, PM Modi referred to the team behind Skyroot Aerospace and said, "The young people working in this 'Skyroot' startup--I have been told the average age of their entire team is only 28 years.""Such youngsters have accomplished this work; I am not talking about any 56-year-old 'youngster,' I am talking about these startups in the country with an average age of 28 that have planted India's flag in space," he said, taking a dig at the Congress leader."I believe that where there are facts, there is no need for chaos. If your logic and facts are strong, there is no need to raise your voice. I hope the discussions in Parliament are conducted with logic and facts. Every voice should find an opportunity to be heard. That is the role of Parliament," he added.He further highlighted India's recent achievements in the space sector and credited the country's young innovators for driving the success."Just before last year's Monsoon Session, an Indian citizen reached the International Space Station, and the day before yesterday, India's young startup achieved a very big milestone. There are very few countries in the world where such private ventures have taken place, and India's youth have taken a new flight into space. It is a very big success," the prime minister said."The benefits of the monsoon are visible, and the Monsoon Session is also commencing. Both the monsoon and the Monsoon Session are very productive; when both are productive, it leads to the welfare of the nation and the welfare of all living beings. Therefore, we pray that both the monsoon and the Monsoon Session remain productive," he added.Appealing to all political parties and MPs to ensure smooth functioning of Parliament, the prime minister also pointed to India's economic resilience despite global challenges.Addressing journalists before the Parliament convened, PM Modi said the conflict in West Asia had created major challenges for India, which depends heavily on imported energy. Despite the crisis, he said, the Indian economy registered a growth rate of 7.7 per cent."We hope that the monsoon and the Monsoon Session of Parliament are productive... I appeal to all MPs to wholeheartedly participate in proceedings of Monsoon session," he added.His remarks came two days after Skyroot Aerospace's Vikram-1 Test Flight-1 successfully reached orbit, marking the maiden flight of India's first privately developed orbital-class rocket.The mission, named Mission Aagaman, was launched from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre. The 24-metre carbon-composite rocket completed all planned flight stages, including stage separations and the firing of its Orbital Adjustment Module (OAM), before placing its payloads into a nearly 450-km orbit. The achievement made India the third country in the world with private orbital launch capability.Earlier, PM Modi had congratulated Skyroot Aerospace on the successful mission, calling it a landmark achievement for India's private space sector.The Monsoon Session of Parliament began on Monday with the Lok Sabha scheduled to take up obituary references, Question Hour, papers to be laid on the Table, a ministerial statement, and the introduction of The Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026, among other listed business.
Article Summary: PM Modi takes apparent swipe at Rahul Gandhi while praising India's young innovators. PM Modi referred to the team behind Skyroot Aerospace and said, "The young people working in this 'Skyroot' startup--I have been told the average age of their entire team is only 28 years"

Article Title: 'Hope for productive monsoon session': PM Modi highlights India's gains ahead of Parliament proceedings
Article Text:
"Country has achieved numerous milestones, a series of accomplishments", PM Modi said ahead of Monsoon session.

NEW DELHI: Prime Minister Narendra Modi Monday addressed the nation ahead of the Monsoon Session of Parliament, expressing optimism for the productive proceedings, which is most likely to be dominated by the issues of donations theft at Ayodhya Ram Mandir , NEET paper leak and the delimitation bill."Be it the monsoon or the Monsoon Session - if both are proactive, they prove to be highly productive. And when both are productive, it leads to the welfare of the nation and the well-being of all living beings. That is why we pray that the monsoon remains both proactive and productive, and that the monsoon session proves productive as well," PM Modi said.Taunting Rahul Gandhi in a veiled attack, PM Modi praised the succcessful mission of Vikram-1 orbital launch vehicle highlighting how the average age of the young Skyroot team is merely 28 years, unlike a "56-year-old youth.""India's youth have embarked on a new journey into space. I was told that the average age of the entire team working at the 'Skyroot' startup is merely 28 years. Young people like these have accomplished this feat. I am not speaking of a 56-year-old youth," PM Modi said.He futher said that the country has "achieved numerous milestones, a series of accomplishments that fill every citizen with pride"."Be it on the national stage, the international arena, or now, even in space, these have truly been moments of pride for India.Just before the monsoon session last year, an Indian citizen reached the International Space Station. And just the other day, a young Indian startup achieved a monumental feat".PM Modi said that a few countries in the world have witnessed such private enterprise," adding that "India's youth have embarked on a new journey into space. It is a tremendous success... India's global profile is gaining universal recognition and acceptance. This is no coincidence; it is a message, a powerful message that the potential and aspirations of our country's youth are as boundless as space itself."PM Modi fruther expressed concern over the West Asia war, sayaing that it posed a major crisis for countries like India that rely on others for energy."Numerous obstacles and crises arose regarding petrol, diesel, LPG, fertilisers, and chemicals. Despite this, India maintained a growth rate of 7.7 per cent, emerging as the fastest-growing major economy in the world. This reflects India's inherent strength and offers a glimpse of the enthusiasm and zeal with which the nation aspires to scale new heights. It is against this backdrop that the Monsoon Session is commencing; the country has gathered momentum, and the spirit of Parliament infuses fresh energy into that pace," he said.He also mentioned Rajasthan's oil refinery and the hydrogen train, praising the achievement as a "testament to the dedication of India's scientists, engineers, innovators, entrepreneurs, and workers, all of whom are living and working with a shared goal for the nation.""It is the result of the country boarding the Reform Express that India's youth are able to show such great courage and achieve success. Recently, a massive oil refinery was dedicated to the nation in Rajasthan. Just a few weeks ago, a semiconductor plant, the third one, was dedicated to the country. Not long ago, a green hydrogen-powered train was dedicated to the citizens; very few countries in the world possess this facility," the PM said.The Monsoon Session of Parliament commences on Monday, with the Lok Sabha scheduled to introduce The Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026, besides taking up obituary references, Question Hour, papers to be laid on the Table, a ministerial statement, and other listed business, according to the Lok Sabha's List of Business.Under legislative business, Union Minister Arjun Ram Meghwal will move for leave to introduce the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026, which seeks to further amend the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Act, 1956. The Bill will replace the ordinance promulgated by the Centre to increase the sanctioned strength of judges in the Supreme Court from 33 to 37.The lower House will also take up a motion regarding the Joint Committee on Offices of Profit, to be moved by BJP MPs Eatala Rajender and Ajay Bhatt.The motion recommends that the Rajya Sabha elect two of its members to the Joint Committee on Offices of Profit through the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote to fill the vacancies caused by the retirement of NR Elango and Deepak Prakash, and communicate the names of the elected members to the Lok Sabha.
Article Summary: Prime Minister Narendra Modi addressed the nation ahead of the Monsoon Session of Parliament. He expressed optimism for the productive proceedings, which is most likely to be dominated by the issues of donations theft at Ayodhya Ram Mandir, NEET paper leak and the delimitation bill.

Article Title: First Cholesterol-Lowering Pill to Replace PCSK9 Injections Gets US Nod
Article Text:
(Representative image) US FDA approves world's first oral PCSK9 inhibitor (Photo: IANS).

NEW DELHI: Patients with difficult-to-control high cholesterol could soon have an oral alternative to powerful cholesterol-lowering injections after the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the world's first oral PCSK9 inhibitor, a class of medicines that dramatically lowers "bad" cholesterol.The once-daily tablet, Lipfendra (enlicitide), has been approved for adults with high cholesterol, including those with an inherited condition called heterozygous familial hypercholesterolaemia (HeFH). The drug is to be used along with diet, exercise and existing cholesterol-lowering treatment. Until now, PCSK9 inhibitors have only been available as injections administered every two or four weeks. However, the drug is not expected to be available in India immediately."Even if a medicine is approved abroad, it cannot be marketed in India automatically. Besides patent-related restrictions, it must obtain approval from the CDSCO after following the prescribed regulatory process, which may include clinical trials as required under Indian regulations. In exceptional cases, patients may seek permission through a separate CDSCO mechanism to import limited quantities for personal treatment.However, medicines cannot simply be brought into the country by friends or relatives," said Dr Subhash Mandal, national general secretary, Indian Pharmaceutical Association (IPA).The approval is significant because many high-risk patients continue to have elevated low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol despite treatment with standard oral medicines. Indian cardiology guidelines recommend aggressive LDL reduction, with targets below 70 mg/dL for high-risk individuals and below 55 mg/dL for those at very high cardiovascular risk, but many patients fail to achieve these goals despite multiple therapies.With cardiovascular disease remaining India's leading killer, cardiologists say better LDL cholesterol control is key to preventing heart attacks and strokes. Clinical trials involving 3,207 adults found the once-daily oral drug reduced LDL cholesterol by 56-59% over 24 weeks in patients already on maximally tolerated statins, delivering reductions comparable to injectable PCSK9 inhibitors and offering another option for those whose cholesterol remains uncontrolled despite existing therapy.However, cardiologists cautioned that the drug is unlikely to replace statins or become a first-line treatment."PCSK9 inhibitors have so far been available only as injections every two or four weeks. The new oral therapy lowers LDL, or 'bad' cholesterol, by about 50-60%, making it a promising option for patients who prefer to avoid injections," said Dr Ambuj Roy, Professor of Cardiology at AIIMS Delhi.He said the drug is likely to be used as a fourth-line therapy, after statins, ezetimibe and bempedoic acid, for patients whose LDL cholesterol remains uncontrolled or who cannot tolerate statins. "While it lowers LDL substantially, we still lack long-term evidence that it reduces heart attacks, bypass surgery, stenting or deaths. It is also expected to be expensive," he said.High LDL cholesterol is a major risk factor for heart disease and stroke. It causes fatty plaques to build up inside arteries, increasing the risk of heart attacks and strokes.
Article Summary: US FDA approves world's first oral PCSK9 inhibitor, a class of medicines that dramatically lowers "bad" cholesterol. Once-daily tablet, Lipfendra (enlicitide), has been approved for adults with high cholesterol. Drug is to be used along with diet, exercise and existing cholesterol-lowering treatment.

Article Title: Sonam Wangchuk health update: Doctors warn of possible organ impact after formation of ketone bodies in urine
Article Text:
(With agency inputs)





NEW DELHI: Educator and climate activist Sonam Wangchuk on Friday said he would "stay alive till July 20 at any cost" as his indefinite hunger strike entered its 20th day, even as doctors warned that his prolonged fast had reached a critical stage.Meanwhile, Wangchuk's health condition showed signs of concern on the 20th day of his hunger strike.Dr Satish Lamba said his health parameters showed that his weight had dropped to 56.55 kg, a reduction of 350 grams over the previous 24 hours."It is the 20th day of the hunger strike. As of 9:30 am on 17 July 2026, the key health parameters are as follows. The person's weight is 56.55 kg, a reduction of 350 grams over the last 24 hours. Blood pressure is 108/68, blood sugar is 70 mg/dL, and the pulse rate is 72 per minute. Hydration is fair, with mild dehydration observed, " Dr Satish Lamba said.Dr Lamba said that prolonged fasting causes changes in the body as it runs out of glucose, proteins and carbohydrates. He explained that the initial stage involves fat loss, followed by muscle loss and the formation of ketone bodies in urine.At the current stage, he said, organ involvement is possible, and the medical team was maintaining 24-hour vigilance and continuous monitoring."As explained earlier, when the body does not receive glucose, protein and carbohydrates, changes occur. The initial stage involved fat loss, followed by muscle loss and the formation of ketone bodies in urine. Now, at this third stage, organ involvement is possible. The medical team is maintaining 24-hour vigilance and continuous monitoring, " Dr Satish Lamba added.The Cockroach Janta Party's (CJP) protest at Jantar Mantar over alleged examination irregularities entered its 28th day, with Wangchuk urging people to participate in large numbers in the outfit's proposed march to Parliament on July 20.Addressing supporters at the protest site, Wangchuk acknowledged that his physical condition was deteriorating but said his determination to continue the fight remained unchanged."I am weak from the outside but very strong inside. I am sure all of you are strong from the inside, and outside too. We need this energy for July 20, when we will take out a peaceful march to Parliament. We will go together and present our plea in the temple of democracy," he said."I will stay alive till July 20 at any cost. If you don't come and July 20 is not successful, I will come back as a ghost," he said in a lighter vein, drawing cheers from the gathering.Meanwhile, senior Congress leader Pawan Khera on Friday met activist Sonam Wangchuk.Khera expressed concern over Wangchuk's health and extended support for his demand for greater accountability and transparency in examinations.Khera, who was among the opposition leaders who met Wangchuk, criticised the government's handling of protests and alleged that it was "insensitive" towards students' concerns."We are all concerned about Sonam Wangchuk's health. We are dealing with a highly insensitive government that fails to understand the language of democratic protest. When facing such a government, the methods of protest must evolve... Risking one's life against this government will not yield results," Khera said.The CJP has been demanding the resignation of Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan and Rs 1 crore compensation for the families of students who allegedly died by suicide amid the examination irregularities row. The outfit has announced a peaceful march to Parliament on July 20, coinciding with the first day of the Monsoon Session.The CJP's protest began on June 20, while Wangchuk joined the agitation on June 28 and has been on an indefinite hunger strike since then.
Article Summary: Sonam Wangchuk's health condition showed signs of concern on the 20th day of his hunger strike. Dr Satish Lamba said that prolonged fasting causes changes in the body as it runs out of glucose, proteins and carbohydrates. At the current stage, organ involvement is possible, he said.

Article Title: Monsoon session begins today as oppn gears up to corner Centre over temple row, NEET, E20
Article Text:
Kiren Rijiju called for consultation instead of a “my way or highway” attitude over the issues to be picked up for debate.

Govt lists 8-bill agenda, silent on delimitation, women's quota

Poll Vote & Share your view What should Parliament focus on first this session? Public concerns Passing key bills 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

NEW DELHI: The stage is set for a stormy Monsoon Session of Parliament from today, with the opposition set to raise several key issues. On Sunday, opposition leaders pushed for discussions on the alleged embezzlement of donations at the Ram temple, the NEET paper leak, the CJP protest at Jantar Mantar, and the E20 petrol row. The government, meanwhile, has cautioned its rivals that any disruption of the legislature is bound to cost them politically.Amid indications that govt is unlikely to allow its rivals to raise in Parliament the theft of temple donations, parliamentary affairs minister Kiren Rijiju called for consultation instead of a “my way or highway” attitude over the issues to be picked up for debate.“Congress has every right to raise its demand, but it can’t force govt,” he said, after Rahul Gandhi and Mallikarjun Kharge wrote to PM Modi, timing their demand for probe and accountability with the session’s start, as several opposition MPs, including SP’s Dharmendra Yadav, raised the issue in an all-party meet led by defence minister Rajnath Singh and attended by 58 MPs of 40 parties.While listing its agenda of eight bills, govt made no mention of its likely move to revive the constitution amendment bill for raising the strength of Lok Sabha and rolling out one-third reservation for women from 2029.Rijiju, though, told reporters later that govt could bring a new item in accordance with the rules after discussing it in the business advisory committee (BAC) during the session, a common practice.He said people had seen that political parties that disrupt Parliament and do not allow discussion end up paying a price, an apparent reminder to BJP's rivals of their poor show in a string of assembly polls, with once-formidable regional parties Trinamool Congress and DMK losing in West Bengal and Tamil Nadu, respectively.With several opposition members raising the NEET-UG paper leak and questioning govt for not engaging with protesters at Jantar Mantar, RSP MP N K Premachandran pushed for a structured debate over the issue, saying paper leaks affect students in huge numbers.As some opposition MPs spoke of the recent fuel price hike, BJP ally and JDU MP Sanjay Jha sought a debate on the West Asia crisis as he credited govt for its energy management, saying it had ensured minimal rise in oil and gas prices compared with other countries and expanded supply for India through alternative sources. India had no role in the crisis, but it handled its adverse impacts deftly, he said.At a BAC meeting later, Speaker Om Birla appealed to all parties for cooperation in ensuring smooth and orderly conduct of the proceedings.
Article Summary: Govt lists 8-bill agenda, silent on delimitation, women's quota. Opposition leaders push for discussions on alleged embezzlement of donations at the Ram temple, the NEET paper leak, the CJP protest at Jantar Mantar.

Article Title: Well-oiled labour supply chain facilitating illegal infiltration of Bangladeshis
Article Text: NEW DELHI: A large majority of Bangladeshi illegal immigrants who sneak into India are not here to organically ‘explore’ economic opportunities; they cross over as part of a well-oiled labour supply chain, with Indian employers in states far from the border relying on headhunting touts in Bangladesh for cheap labour.The ‘jobs for illegal immigrants’ eco-system has touts at every stage — facilitating infiltration, transportation to job sites and arranging forged documentation before settling them into jobs across sectors such as textile manufacturing, construction and pharmaceuticals.“It’s an organised eco-system that comprises Bangladeshi recruitment touts who, based on requirements of Indian employers for cheap labour, identify unemployed Bangladeshis seeking low-paid, unskilled jobs in India. Every step, from their illegal entry into India to reaching their job locations, is planned well in advance,” a senior Assam govt official told TOI.The official said infiltration guides help the selected Bangladeshis cross over, mostly through Meghalaya, Tripura and West Bengal. Agents on the Indian side arrange their transit to Assam and onward train journeys from Guwahati to job locations across the country.Local touts help them secure forged documents like Aadhaar before another set of agents hand them over to Indian employers willing to hire them at wages lower than those payable to Indians.Assam, which recently busted one such tout network transporting illegal immigrants from Guwahati railway station to Tamil Nadu to work in a textile factory, leading to 34 arrests, raised the issue at the all-India conference of SPs of border districts chaired by home minister Amit Shah on July 9. The state sought a legal framework to hold Indian employers exploiting the Bangladeshi illegal immigration network accountable.Intelligence sources said Bangladeshi headhunters work on labour demand conveyed by agents linked to Indian employers. The network also arranges forged Aadhaar and PAN cards in destination states, enabling illegal immigrants to register as workers and move between work sites.“Armed with the same documents, many went on to project themselves as Indian citizens and even enrolled as voters. Special intensive revision (SIR), however, may have cut their dream run short by requiring proof of their parents’ or relatives’ presence in the 2002 rolls,” a police officer told TOI.
Article Summary: The ‘jobs for illegal immigrants’ eco-system has touts at every stage — facilitating infiltration, transportation to job sites and arranging forged documentation. Assam, which recently busted one such tout network, raised the issue at the all-India conference of SPs of border districts chaired by home minister Amit Shah.

Article Title: DMK confirms TOI report it’s open to rethink on delimit bill
Article Text:
Lok Sabha (File photo)

NEW DELHI: Let govt bring the bill on raising the strength of Lok Sabha, then DMK will spell out its stand, the regional party with a crucial bloc of 22 MPs in the House said Sunday, confirming that the party is willing to revisit its stand on delimitation if its provisions do not harm the interest of southern states.Senior DMK functionary Tiruchi Siva said govt should first make it clear as to what is on its mind as his party wants more clarity on the matter. TOI had exclusively reported Saturday that DMK is open to negotiation with govt if it is given an ironclad guarantee that the interests of southern states will not be adversely affected.“I want to underscore if southern states are affected, then we will not accept it in any manner. Our representation in Parliament should remain...,” he said in what appeared to be his pitch for maintaining the existing share of strength of southern states. As part of INDIA bloc in the last Parliament session, DMK was at the forefront of protest against the constitution amendment bill, which was eventually defeated as a joint opposition denied a two-thirds majority govt needed for its passage.DMK is no longer part of INDIA bloc as its ally Congress joined hands with actor Vijay-led TVK, which trounced the M K Stalin-led party in the Tamil Nadu assembly polls, giving rise to the possibility that it may be open to business with BJP on renegotiated terms.Asked about Shah’s assurance, Siva said, “He (Shah) had said he will bring a bill. Let the bill come out and we will spell out DMK’s stand.”To a question about his stand if the seats are raised by 50% in all states, he said he will not react to speculation.
Article Summary: DMK is open to negotiation with govt if it is given an ironclad guarantee that the interests of southern states will not be adversely affected. To a question about his stand if the seats are raised by 50% in all states, he said he will not react to speculation.

Article Title: Opposition protests invite to TMC rebels to all-party meeting
Article Text: NEW DELHI: In solidarity with the Mamata Banerjee-headed Trinamool Congress, opposition parties on Sunday staged a rare walkout from the all-party meeting to protest the govt’s invite to its breakaway faction, even as both warring groups took potshots at one another, foretelling a fiery session of Parliament from Monday.Govt, however, defended its decision, asserting that it cannot deny space to the faction of 20 MPs who have approached Speaker Om Birla for recognising their merger with Nationalist Citizens Party of India (NCPI) and have already been allotted separate seats in Lok Sabha.“It is govt’s duty to invite every group,” parliamentary affairs minister Kiren Rijiju told reporters. Playing down the walkout, he said it was only for a brief period as opposition parties rejoined the meeting soon.Facing disintegration and an existential crisis, TMC has leaned on other opposition parties, including Congress, which used to get short shrift from the party in Parliament when Mamata was West Bengal’s chief minister, to strengthen its cause.John Brittas of CPM, which has been a bitter critic of Mamata, said they supported the party in the walkout in the interests of parliamentary democracy which is under “attack” from BJP.As Mahua Moitra, who is among the eight Lok Sabha MPs siding with Mamata, called the majority bloc of 20 MPs “traitors” and demanded their disqualification, Sudip Bandyopadhyay, the chosen leader of breakaway faction, retorted that a “small” party with only eight MPs no longer deserves front row seating in House.Mahua was joined by members of other opposition parties as she slammed the invitation to Sudip as the NCPI leader and to Kakoli Ghosh Dastidar as its chief whip and claimed that they cannot be recognised under anti-defection law. Only original party can merge with another party, she added.
Article Summary: Opposition parties staged a rare walkout from the all-party meeting to protest the govt’s invite to its breakaway faction. Govt, however, defended its decision, asserting that it cannot deny space to the faction of 20 MPs.

Article Title: New bills, old battles, changed equations: What awaits Parliament this Monsoon Session
Article Text:
What's on agenda

Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Bill, 2026

Income-tax (Amendment) Bill, 2026

Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026

Registration of Births and Deaths (Amendment) Bill, 2026

Prevention of Insults to National Honour (Amendment) Bill, 2026

Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development (Amendment) Bill, 2026

Opposition's playbook

Where Parliament left things

How things changed

Return of the delimitation bill

How numbers stack up

Where NCP, Sena (UBT) and DMK stand

Poll Vote & Share your view Do you support the Women Reservation Bill linked to the Delimitation Bill? Yes, it is essential for women's representation No, it undermines federalism 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

The final hurdle

NEW DELHI: Parliament's Monsoon Session, beginning Monday, could provide the Narendra Modi government a second shot at passing its flagship Delimitation Bill linked to the Women's Reservation Bill after the NDA government suffered its first floor defeat in Parliament since 2014 during the Budget Session earlier this year.Much has changed in the three months since. A bruised opposition has been hit by electoral defeats, defections and the collapse of key alliances, while the NDA has bolstered its numbers - changing the arithmetic ahead of what could be one of the session's biggest legislative battles.The government has listed a packed legislative agenda for the Monsoon Session.The key Bills slated for consideration or introduction include:Among them, the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Bill is expected to trigger the sharpest political debates.The FCRA amendment seeks to strengthen transparency and accountability in the receipt and utilisation of foreign funds. However, it has emerged as a politically sensitive issue ahead of the Kerala Assembly elections, with opposition parties alleging that the proposed changes could disproportionately impact Christian organisations and NGOs that receive foreign contributions.Besides these, the government is expected to push the Income-tax (Amendment) Bill to replace an Ordinance, legislation to increase the sanctioned strength of Supreme Court judges from 33 to 37, amendments to tighten norms for delayed registration of births and deaths, changes to the Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, and a Bill to modernise the MSME law by strengthening mechanisms for delayed payments, enforcing arbitral awards and easing business regulations.Meanwhile, the opposition is expected to corner the government on a range of political and governance issues. The first signs of a stormy session emerged on Sunday, when the entire opposition staged a symbolic walkout from an all-party meeting convened ahead of the Monsoon Session. The protest was triggered by the invitation extended to rebel TMC MPs and demands for discussions on the alleged Ram temple donation theft, the NEET paper leak and ethanol blending in petrol.On the temple donation embezzlement row, Congress along with the opposition parties has indicated that they will raise the issue in both Houses, seeking accountability from the government.The Centre, however, is likely to maintain that the matter falls within the jurisdiction of the Uttar Pradesh government.Outside Parliament, pressure on the Centre continues to mount as the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) has called for a march to Parliament on Monday over the alleged NEET irregularities.The protest gathered momentum after activist Sonam Wangchuk was taken to hospital by Delhi Police during his hunger strike, with demonstrators reinforcing their demand for the resignation of education minister Pradhan.The issue is expected to spill over into Parliament, where opposition parties are likely to press the government for answers.In the Budget Session, the government failed to secure the two-thirds majority required to pass the Constitution Amendment Bill.The bill sought to enable fresh delimitation to operationalise the 33 per cent women's reservation quota in Parliament. It proposed amending Article 81 to increase the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha from 550 to 850 members.However, regional parties in the opposition accused the government of undermining federalism by implementing delimitation through the back door.They argued that if Lok Sabha seats were redistributed solely on the basis of the 2011 Census, the southern states' share in the House would shrink, pushing them towards irrelevance in national politics.Prime Minister Narendra Modi, however, asserted that adding 33 per cent more seats for women in the Lok Sabha would not alter the existing proportion of seats allocated to states."The proportion of seats will not change; the increase will be in the same proportion. If you want a guarantee or a promise, I will say so. If there is some good word in Tamil, I will use it. When the intention is good, we do not need to play with words," PM Modi had said in Parliament.Just after the conclusion of the Budget Session, things went downhill for the opposition bloc. Regional constituents of the INDIA bloc had to fight one battle after another.In the assembly elections concluded in May, the Trinamool Congress was uprooted by the BJP in West Bengal, while the DMK lost to the TVK. Also, the Congress replaced the Left government in Kerala.Things did not stop falling apart for these parties there. The poll results triggered an exodus in the TMC as 20 of its 28 MPs rebelled against party supremo Mamata Banerjee and merged with the previously obscure Nationalist Citizens Party of India — now the second-biggest constituent of the National Democratic Alliance in the Lok Sabha.Meanwhile, the "iron-clad" alliance between the Congress and the DMK was shattered after a fractured mandate in the Tamil Nadu polls. Congress extended support to actor Vijay-led TVK after it fell short of the magic number to form the government, leading the DMK to snap ties with the INDIA bloc.To make things even worse, Shiv Sena (UBT) suffered another vertical split after 2022. Six of its nine MPs decided to jump ship and join rival Eknath Shinde's faction.Although the government has not listed a fresh Constitution Amendment Bill on women's reservation and delimitation, it is hoping to succeed where it failed in the Budget Session.With a bruised Opposition reduced to a shadow of what it was three months ago and the NDA's tally strengthened, the government is now considering "suitable adjustments" to the bill to garner support from regional parties across the opposition.The Lok Sabha has an effective strength of 540 members, meaning a Constitutional Amendment Bill requires the support of at least 360 MPs to pass.In April, the bill received 298 votes in favour and 230 against.The NDA currently has the backing of 293 MPs. With the support of 20 NCPI MPs and six Shiv Sena (UBT) MPs, the ruling alliance's tally rises to 319.The YSRCP, which has four MPs in the Lok Sabha, has also reiterated its support to the government, taking the tally to 323."Our leader YS Jagan Mohan Reddy has clearly stated that we will support the Women's Reservation Bill and the Delimitation Bill. We have already supported it. If these bills are introduced during this session, we will support them," YSRCP MP PV Midhun Reddy said."As for other bills, we will make a decision only after examining their content. If they are in the interest of our state and its people, we will certainly support them; however, if they are not, we will raise the issue in the House and oppose those bills," he added.Meanwhile, opposition parties such as the NCP (SP), Shiv Sena (UBT) and the DMK have indicated they could support the bill if their concerns are addressed.NCP (SP) working president Supriya Sule said if the Delimitation Bill proposes a uniform 50 per cent increase in Lok Sabha seats across all states, "there would be little reason to oppose it." If Sharad Pawar's party, which has eight MPs, extends support to the Constitutional Amendment Bill, the NDA-backed tally could rise to 331.Similarly, Shiv Sena (UBT) MP Sanjay Raut said the opposition could reconsider its stand if the government's proposed amendments address its concerns. "We will oppose the Delimitation Bill, but if necessary amendments as suggested by us are made, then the opposition can think it over," Raut said. This would add another three MPs, taking the tally to 334.Speculation is also rife that the DMK, with its 22 MPs, could support the government following its messy breakup with Congress.DMK leader Tiruchi Siva said that the party is in favour of the Women's Reservation Bill, but it wants more clarity on the delimitation issue."The DMK is in favour of the Women's Reservation Bill on the current strength in the Lok Sabha, but we want more clarity on the delimitation issue," he told reporters after the meeting.Speaking to The Times of India earlier, a senior DMK functionary said the party was open to negotiations if the government guaranteed that the interests of Tamil Nadu and other southern states would not be compromised by delimitation and clearly spelt out each state's share in advance."The government should explain the concrete share of each state to rule out any possibility of the southern states losing out, and that can be the basis for talks," the functionary said, adding that no formal consultation with the government had taken place yet. If the DMK also backs the bill, the tally would rise to 356.Even if the government manages to secure the support of all the parties currently seen as potential backers, it would still fall around four votes short of the two-thirds majority required.A two-thirds majority is not a fixed number in practical terms. Under parliamentary rules, it is calculated on the basis of members present and voting, not the total strength of the House. This means the threshold falls if some MPs abstain from voting.Against this backdrop, the governenment is hopeful that some non-NDA MPs may also abstain when the bill is put to vote, potentially lowering the number of votes required to secure the necessary two-thirds majority.If reports are to be believed, the BJP is also attempting to encourage cross-voting from the opposition benches.According to reports, the party has also been reaching out to several opposition MPs in the hope of securing support beyond the NDA's formal numbers.Congress, on the other hand, has also been reaching out to various INDIA bloc parties to ascertain the numbers the opposition alliance is left with following the TMC and Shiv Sena (UBT) defections.
Article Summary: Parliament's Monsoon Session, beginning Monday, could provide the Narendra Modi government a second shot at passing its flagship Delimitation Bill. The NDA government suffered its first floor defeat in Parliament since 2014 during the Budget Session earlier this year. A bruised opposition has been hit by electoral defeats, defections and the collapse of key alliances.

Article Title: Government may seek Parliamentary nod for additional spending
Article Text: NEW DELHI: With the fertiliser subsidy, other West Asia related spends and initiatives, such as the electronics manufacturing push through schemes for semiconductors and mobiles adding to the Centre’s expenditure, finance minister Nirmala Sitharaman may seek Parliamentary approval for additional spending, without losing sight of the overall fiscal situation.The discussion around the need for Parliamentary sanction is in contrast to her ministry’s earlier stance as it wanted to wait for more data to come before assessing the situation. With the turmoil in West Asia still to settle, it is tough to have a clear picture, but officials indicated that the spending so far is higher and some of the expenditure, which will be matched through savings elsewhere, will need legislative clearance.So far, GST and direct tax revenues have been on track and Sitharaman is pushing the department of investment and public asset management (DIPAM) hard to provide some cushion through additional realisation. Her meetings with the DIPAM brass have helped the Centre rake in Rs 20,272 crore — a little over one-fourth of the target with more than two-thirds of the fiscal year left.A huge comfort will come if DIPAM manages to move ahead and finalise the IDBI Bank transaction, which has been in the pipeline for nearly five years.With just two players in the fray, a lot will depend on how Emirates NDB, which has already acquired a majority stake in RBL Bank.Prem Vatsa’s Fairfax, the other suitor, has a 40% stake in Catholic Syrian Bank and RBI will have a role to play in deciding its fate.For the moment, it is the additional expenditure, which has prompted a rethink on the first supplementary demand for grants as the higher fertiliser subsidy bill needs to be settled. While the fertiliser department had sought doubling of the allocation, prices have softened over the last few weeks, compared with the peak, proving some relief. The oil subsidy, which too has soared, will also need to be settled, although not immediately.The Rs 1.9 lakh crore push for electronics manufacturing will require some additional allocation this year and with other ministries also drawing up replacement schemes for production linked incentive, some of which may have to wait, given the pressure on expenditure.For a govt that has stuck to the fiscal deficit targets, despite facing one pressure or another almost every year, Sitharaman will seek to maintain her record and achieve the goal of 4.3% of GDP.
Article Summary: Finance minister Nirmala Sitharaman may seek Parliamentary approval for additional spending. The fertiliser subsidy, other West Asia related spends and initiatives, such as the electronics manufacturing push through schemes for semiconductors and mobiles adding to the Centre’s expenditure.

Article Title: NEET cutoff at record high, qualifiers shrink by 1.1 lakh+
Article Text:
Highest Success Rate In EWS; 9% Skipped Retest

Highest Success Rate In EWS; 9% Skipped Retest

NEW DELHI: At 213 marks, NEET-UG recorded its highest ever qualifying cutoff, even as the number of candidates clearing the medical entrance examination fell by 1.15 lakh, a divergence driven by a smaller-appearing pool and a sharp upward shift in the score distribution. Moreover, the total qualified candidates for EWS and OBC for the first time breached 60% of their 'appeared' cohort, and the percentage of unreserved qualifiers now rests below 50%. The qualifying cutoffs for unreserved and EWS candidates rose to 213 from 144 in 2025 - a 69-mark jump. For OBC, SC/ST candidates, the threshold increased by 64 marks, from 113 to 177.The UR/EWS qualifying range moved from 720-162 in 2024 to 686-144 in 2025 and 715-213 this year. For OBC, SC and ST candidates, the lower end moved from 127 in 2024 to 113 last year before surging to 177.Registrations remained virtually unchanged at 22.8 lakh, compared with 22.76 lakh in 2025. But only 19.99 lakh candidates appeared, down from 22.09 lakh - a decline of nearly 2.1 lakh. Attendance consequently dropped from 97.1% to 87.7%. The number qualifying fell from 12.37 lakh to 11.21 lakh, a decline of 9.3%.Yet the success rate among those who appeared remained virtually unchanged at about 56.1%, showing that the fall in qualifiers was largely an absentee story rather than a collapse in performance. Under the category-wise qualifying criteria, UR and EWS candidates must secure at least the 50th percentile, while OBC, SC and ST candidates require the 40th percentile. For candidates with benchmark disabilities, the qualifying requirement is 45th percentile for UR/EWS and 40th percentile for OBC, SC and ST.In marks, the 2026 ranges were 715-213 for UR/EWS, 212-177 for OBC, SC and ST, 212-194 for UR/EWS-PwBD and 193-177 for most reserved-category PwBD candidates. The ST-PwBD range was 191-177. NTA's table shows 9.97 lakh candidates clearing at the UR/EWS threshold, while 81,111 OBC, 29,947 SC and 12,452 ST candidates qualified within the lower score band. These are score-band figures and not the total number qualifying from each social category.Separately, category-wise results show that 61.8% of EWS candidates who appeared qualified, the highest rate, followed by 60.7% of OBC candidates. The qualification rate was 52.4% among SC candidates, 51.2% among general candidates and 49% among ST candidates.
Article Summary: At 213 marks, NEET-UG recorded its highest ever qualifying cutoff. The qualifying cutoffs for unreserved and EWS candidates rose to 213 from 144 in 2025. For OBC, SC/ST candidates, the threshold increased by 64 marks, from 113 to 177.

Article Title: Bill seeks 3-year jail for disturbing rendition of ‘Vande Mataram’
Article Text: NEW DELHI: Anyone who prevents or disrupts the singing of Vande Mataram, which must now be sung in full at all state events and civil investiture ceremonies, could face up to three years in jail, a fine, or both under a Bill the govt plans to introduce in Parliament’s Monsoon session starting Monday.The Prevention of Insults to National Honour (Amendment) Bill, 2026, proposes to amend Section 3 of the relevant Act, which at present deals only with insults to the National Anthem , to add the national song to its ambit.“Whoever, intentionally, prevents the singing of the national anthem or the national song; or causes disturbance to any assembly engaged in such singing, shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both,” states Section 3 of the proposed bill.Where a person has already been convicted, every subsequent offence shall be punishable with a minimum jail term of one year.The statement of objects and reasons for 2026 amendment bill cites the Jan 24, 1950, meeting of the Constituent Assembly, where its president Rajendra Prasad had stated that Vande Mataram, “which played a historic role in the Indian freedom struggle, shall be honoured equally with ‘Jana-Gana-Mana’ and shall have equal status with it”.“Presently, there is no specific legal provision to prevent insults to the singing of ‘Vande Mataram’,” the statement points out while justifying the need for penal provision.
Article Summary: Vande Mataram must now be sung in full at all state events and civil investiture ceremonies. Anyone who prevents or disrupts the singing of Vande Mataram could face up to three years in jail, a fine, or both under a Bill the govt plans to introduce in Parliament.

Article Title: 'Misleading the families?' AI-171 victims' kin seek black box data, memorial after AAIB affidavit
Article Text:
Air India AI-171 crash

NEW DELHI: The families of those killed in the Air India AI-171 crash have stepped up demands for the release of the aircraft's black box data and cockpit voice recorder (CVR) transcript, questioning the aircraft accident investigation bureau's (AAIB) decision to withhold the material while promising only a final investigation report.According to news agency PTI, the demands were raised at a meeting of victims' families in Ahmedabad on Sunday, days after the AAIB informed the Supreme Court that it would not disclose black box data or cockpit voice recorder recordings during the ongoing investigation.The London Gatwick-bound Air India flight AI-171 crashed shortly after take-off from Ahmedabad's Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel International Airport on June 12, 2025, when the Boeing 787 Dreamliner struck the BJ Medical College hostel complex. The crash killed 241 of the 242 people on board and 19 people on the ground, making it India's deadliest aviation disaster in decades.Romin Vahora, who lost his brother, niece and aunt in the tragedy, questioned the AAIB's decision to withhold the cockpit recordings. "If they never intended to release the data, why did they release a cockpit voice recording transcript (as part of preliminary report) just a few days after the crash?", he asked.Vahora alleged that the families wanted to know whether the selective disclosure was intended to "shift the blame onto the pilots or mislead the victims' families", PTI reported.Seeking greater transparency, he said the families wanted the raw black box data to be made public, shared directly with them or provided to their legal representatives.Vahora said the families had not initiated legal proceedings and were awaiting the final investigation report, while their lawyers continued exploring legal options to secure access to the black box data. He added that if the report was not released within the timeline indicated by the AAIB, the families would consider holding protests after obtaining the necessary permissions.Last week, the AAIB told the Supreme Court that the draft final report into the June 12 crash was expected to be ready in October. In its affidavit, the agency said there was an "absolute statutory prohibition" on releasing cockpit voice recordings and airborne image recordings, arguing that witness statements, cockpit voice recorder transcripts, air traffic control communications and medical records must remain confidential to protect the integrity of the investigation and future aviation safety inquiries.The victims' families also renewed their demand for a permanent memorial at the crash site. Vahora urged the government, Air India and the Tata group to earmark land and allocate funds for a monument honouring those who lost their lives.
Article Summary: Families of those killed in the Air India AI-171 crash have stepped up demands for the release of the aircraft's black box data and cockpit voice recorder (CVR) transcript. The demands were raised at a meeting of victims' families in Ahmedabad on Sunday. The families also renewed their demand for a permanent memorial at the crash site.

Article Title: Even as fertility rates drop, young still aspire to partnership, parenthood, shows UN report
Article Text: NEW DELHI: A first-of-its-kind survey of young adults (aged 18-39) across 73 countries, including India, by United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) shows that even as much of the world’s marriage rates have declined and fertility has fallen to historically low levels, young people are not turning away from relationships or family life. Marriage, it says, remains “a common ideal relationship” arrangement among surveyed young adults.The report highlights that across countries — with very different demographic realities — many continue to aspire to partnership and parenthood, but say their decisions are increasingly shaped by financial security, stable employment, housing, gender equality and confidence in the future.UNFPA’s ‘Lives, Choices and Futures — Demographic Futures Survey’ 2026’ gathered perspectives of more than 1,08,000 internet connected young adults, including 1,722 respondents in India.More than two thirds of respondents selected an ideal relationship and living arrangement that includes marriage — 36% before living together and 34% after a period of cohabitation. The survey says 16% reported remaining single as their preferred living arrangement.Its findings gain significance, as across much of the world partnership formation is occurring later, marriage rates have declined and fertility has fallen to historically low levels.“Two thirds of the world’s population now lives in a country or area where the total fertility rate is below 2.1 children per woman — the level commonly associated with the population size remaining stable over time,” the survey says.Among respondents aged 25–39, who ideally wish to marry or live with a partner, a quarter are currently single and not dating. The proportion is higher among male respondents (30% than among female respondents (19%).Financial security is the highest-rated consideration for partnership formation, cited as important by 81% of respondents. It turns out that economic and housing constraints constitute the most commonly reported category of barrier to partnership, rated important by 57%.The survey finds that among respondents — currently seeking a partner — personal networks, school or work environments, and online platforms are the most commonly reported channels for meeting potential partners, each reported by approximately one third of respondents. Overall the survey says more than 40% of respondents report spending over two hours a day on social media or the Internet for entertainment, making it the most time-intensive form of online activity examined.In India, while more than eight in 10 respondents (83%) said they feel positive or very positive about the future, nearly half of those surveyed (47%) said they are very worried about conflict, economic insecurity and inequality.While across most regional groupings of countries, two children is the most commonly reported ideal family size, the mean number of children reported by respondents aged 35–39 is lower than the mean ideal number of children reported by respondents aged 18–39.For instance, in the case of India, women aged 18-39 reported an ideal family size of 2.1 children, compared with an average of one child among women aged 35-39. Men reported an ideal family size of 2.2 children, compared with an average of 1.1 children among men aged 35-39.Parenthood is part of the future plans of most surveyed in India. Among respondents aged 35-39 in India who do not currently have children, nearly 85% said they would ideally like to become parents.
Article Summary: UNFPA’s ‘Lives, Choices and Futures — Demographic Futures Survey’ 2026’ gathered perspectives of more than 1,08,000 internet connected young adults, including 1,722 respondents in India. More than two thirds of respondents selected an ideal relationship and living arrangement that includes marriage.

Article Title: Less than half of 483 Lok Sabha MPs attended 90-100% of sittings in Parliament’s budget session, shows analysis
Article Text: NEW DELHI: As parties gear-up for taking up a wide range of issues during Parliament’s monsoon session — expected to be fiery in light of Ram temple and NEET-UG paper leak issues — the presence of MPs in both Houses is most crucial. An analysis of attendance record of the previous budget session, which ended in April, by NGO Commonwealth Human Rights Initiative shows that it is the smaller parties — with a handful of MPs — that have the highest average attendance record figures.Overall in Lok Sabha, as many as 101 MPs had signed the attendance register for all 31 sittings of the budget session — less than a quarter, or just about 21% of the 483 MPs whose attendance record was publicly available.In terms of average attendance record of parties in LS, RLP — that has one MP (Hanuman Beniwal from Nagaur — and RLD — that has two MPs (Chandan Chauhan from Bijnor and Rajkumar Sangwan from Baghpat — registered 100% attendance record for all 31 sittings. CPI(ML-L) stood second — with two of its MPs attending 30 out of the 31 sittings — as did Zoram People’s Movement with its lone MPs. Samajwadi Party took third position — with 37 of its MPs averaging attendance of 28.43 days.Overall less than one half (45.7%) of the 483 LS members had attended between 90-100% of the sittings or at least signed the attendance register. The report examines the attendance record as available on the website of both Houses for the budget session to spot political party-wise trends and patterns.The single-largest party in LS, BJP (with details of 191 members available) averaged only 26.46 sittings per MP. Congress — single-largest party in opposition — averaged only 23.13 sittings (with details of 97 MPs available). Among regional political parties with more than 20 MPs during the budget session, TMC averaged only 15 sittings and DMK with 22 MPs even lesser with 14.64 sittings.In Rajya Sabha, 119 MPs — less than 46.6% of 255 MPs had attended between 90-100% of the sittings or at least signed the attendance register for those many days.The top 10 political parties clocking the highest percentage of attendance are regional parties. DMDK with its lone MP (LK Sudhish) is the only one who recorded 100% attendance, followed by J&K-NC with three MPs clocking an average of 94.62%. MPs from JDS, MNF, BJD, SP, NPP and SS all recorded an average of more than 90% attendance.Despite being the largest party in the House, BJP recorded an average attendance of only 84.39% followed by Congress recording 80.66%, the report stated.
Article Summary: As many as 101 MPs had signed the attendance register for all 31 sittings of the budget session. Less than a quarter, or just about 21% of the 483 MPs whose attendance record was publicly available. RLP — that has one MP (Hanuman Beniwal from Nagaur — and RLD — that. has two MPs (Chandan Chauhan from Bijnor and Rajkumar Sangwan from Baghpat — registered 100% attendance record.

Article Title: ‘People’s safety our highest priority’: Assam govt requests Army, Air Force to remain on standby for flood evacuations
Article Text:
Morigaon: People carrying their belongings cross a flood-affected area on a boat, in Morigaon district, Assam. (PTI Photo)

(With PTI inputs)

NEW DELHI: With parts of Assam continuing to reel under floods, chief minister Himanta Biswa Sarma on Sunday said the state government has requested the Army and the Air Force to remain on standby to assist in evacuating people from flood-hit districts.Sarma added that he has instructed four cabinet colleagues to rush to Upper Assam to oversee rescue and relief efforts in the three districts hit by the latest wave of floods."As the flood situation in Sivasagar, Charaideo and Jorhat continues to worsen, the Government of Assam has requested the Indian Army and the Indian Air Force to be on standby to support evacuation and rescue operations, if required," he posted.He directed ministers Ajanta Neog, Bimal Borah, Keshab Mahanta and Susanta Borgohain to immediately reach the affected areas to supervise relief efforts and ensure timely assistance reaches every affected family."The safety and well-being of our people is our highest priority. I am monitoring the situation round the clock, and we will take every necessary step to protect lives and provide all possible relief," Sarma added.Meanwhile, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has predicted more rain across the northeastern states, including Assam, over the next few days.With heavy rain forecast for Guwahati and several other Assam districts over the next three to four days, the Assam State Disaster Management Authority (ASDMA) also urged residents to remain alert.“Daily commuters and long-distance travellers are requested to plan their routines accordingly, avoiding unnecessary movement during periods of intense rainfall.Residents in low-lying and hillside areas should stay alert for any signs of flooding or landslides,” ASDMA said in an advisory on Sunday.The body also asked local authorities to allow employees to leave early if required. It warned that moderate to heavy rain of 6cm to 12cm in 24 hours, along with thunderstorms and lightning, could worsen waterlogging, flooding, flash floods, traffic snarls and localised landslides in vulnerable areas.
Article Summary: Chief minister Himanta Biswa Sarma has asked the Army and the Air Force to remain on standby to assist in evacuating people from flood-hit districts. He has instructed four cabinet colleagues to rush to Upper Assam to oversee rescue and relief efforts in the three districts hit by the latest wave of floods.

Article Title: 'Parliament should discuss if new anti-defection law needed': Congress' Manish Tewari after TMC, Shiv Sena (UBT) splits
Article Text:
Congress MP Manish Tewari (File photo/PTI)

NEW DELHI: Congress MP Manish Tewari on Monday called for a discussion on whether there should be a new anti-defection law, saying the current composition of the Lok Sabha no longer reflects the mandate given by voters in the 2024 parliamentary elections."The fundamental point is that in 2024, the people gave a mandate, and the Lok Sabha was constituted in accordance with that mandate. Today, two years later, the composition of the Lok Sabha is no longer in line with that mandate. It is contrary to the people's mandate," PTI quoted Tewari as saying.Speaking ahead of the Monsoon Session of Parliament, Tewari said the "foremost priority" of the session should be for Parliament to set aside other business and debate whether a new anti-defection law is needed.The Chandigarh MP stressed the need to curb the "opportunistic politics" often described as "Aaya Ram, Gaya Ram", where politicians switch parties without regard for ideological or policy differences.Tewari also emphasised the need to distinguish and address genuine ideological or policy disagreements."A balance needs to be struck both inside and outside Parliament and state assemblies, because all these recent developments have worked to weaken Indian democracy," he said.In recent days, two parties opposed to the ruling BJP — the Trinamool Congress (TMC) and Shiv Sena (UBT) — have witnessed splits within their ranks, with some MPs either extending support to the BJP-led National Democratic Alliance (NDA) or joining parties already part of the NDA.In the TMC's case, three of its Rajya Sabha MPs resigned from the party, joined the BJP, and were later re-elected as BJP candidates. Meanwhile, 20 of the TMC's 28 Lok Sabha MPs merged their faction with the little-known Nationalist Citizens Party of India and extended support to the NDA.From Shiv Sena (UBT), six of its Lok Sabha MPs joined the Eknath Shinde-led Shiv Sena, a BJP ally, reducing the Uddhav Thackeray-led party's strength in the Lower House to three members.In the 2024 Lok Sabha elections, the NDA won 293 seats in the 543-member House, including 240 seats secured by the BJP. The recent developments have further increased its numbers.
Article Summary: Congress MP Manish Tewari called for a discussion on whether there should be a new anti-defection law. He said the current composition of the Lok Sabha no longer reflects the mandate given by voters in the 2024 parliamentary elections. TEWari stressed the need to curb the "opportunistic politics" often described as "Aaya Ram, Gaya Ram"

Article Title: ‘No state has witnessed such a political change since Independence’: Amit Shah on Bengal
Article Text:
Union home minister Amit Shah in Kolkata

NEW DELHI: Union home minister Amit Shah on Sunday described the change of guard in West Bengal as "unlike any political change since 1947", months after his Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) ended the Trinamool Congress' (TMC) 15-year rule in the state."The change in Bengal is unlike any political change witnessed in any state across the country since Independence," Shah said in Kolkata while virtually laying the foundation stone for a curd production plant under the Amul Bengal Dairy Project."West Bengal was once a beacon for the entire country in every field. In education, West Bengal remained decades ahead of the rest of the nation. Whether it was the freedom struggle or the country's cultural and spiritual renaissance, Bengal gave India some of its greatest leaders and freedom fighters," he added.Shah described the Left Front, which ruled West Bengal for over 30 years (1977-2011), as a "foreign ideology" and the TMC, which was in power from 2011 until it was voted out in April this year, as "corrupt and ideologically bankrupt.""First Bengal was ruled by the foreign ideology of the Left for three decades, then by the corrupt and ideologically bankrupt TMC for 15 years," the minister added in a sharp attack on the previous governments in the state.The Amul Bengal Dairy Project, he noted, would mark a milestone for the state's dairy sector and boost the income of milk producers.The Rs 700 crore project will process 30 lakh litres of milk daily and produce 10 lakh kg of curd and other cultured dairy products every day. It is expected to benefit more than 1.25 lakh dairy farmers in West Bengal, including over 30,000 women, while strengthening the cooperative dairy network in eastern India.During his three-day visit to West Bengal, Shah held discussions with the Centre and the state government officials on governance issues, including measures to curb infiltration, strengthen law and order and improve women's safety.The event marked the conclusion of Shah's three-day visit to West Bengal, during which he attended official programmes and held meetings on key issues.
Article Summary: Union home minister Amit Shah described the change of guard in West Bengal as "unlike any political change since 1947" Shah was laying the foundation stone for a curd production plant under the Amul Bengal Dairy Project. The event marked the conclusion of Shah's three-day visit to West Bengal, during which he attended official programmes.

Article Title: Who will care for the child? Why many women remain outside the workforce
Article Text:
The motherhood dilemma

What the numbers say



Women and childcare

Tale of two cities



Where women earn the most

The missing childcare support



Several tasks women do

Missing childcare infrastructure

The gender gap at work



Why it matters beyond households



Women in workforce

What the data shows



Here are what infrastructural changes needed

Can you become an IAS officer while being an 'expert mother'?Can women pursue demanding careers while also fulfilling the responsibilities of motherhood? Uttar Pradesh governor Anandiben Patel believes they can and should.“Whether you become an IAS officer or a teacher, first become an expert mother. Everyone should know how to cook the food prepared at home,” Patel said at the 41st convocation of Chhatrapati Shahu Ji Maharaj University.Her remarks, part of a wider message on education, family and parenting, have revived a familiar debate: what support do women have to balance careers and caregiving?Labour data suggests this balance remains difficult. For many urban women, childcare and household responsibilities, not a lack of education or ambition, remain the biggest barriers to paid work. National Statistics Office survey covering India’s 46 largest urban centres found that childcare and household responsibilities keep 69% of non-working women out of the labour force. For many women, unpaid care work remains a greater barrier to employment than education or qualifications.The gender divide is stark. Barely 1% of non-working men cite childcare or housework as the reason they are outside the workforce. Among women, it is by far the most common explanation.Even higher education hasn't solved the problem. More than 60% of women graduates are neither working nor looking for work. Only around one in ten unemployed female graduates is actively searching for a job, compared with nearly eight in ten men.For many women, the challenge isn't capability—it's compatibility between career and caregiving responsibilities.That is something Nibha Singh Mahar, an English teacher at an Army School in Ranikhet, knows firsthand. Married at 23, she became a mother at 25 and stepped away from her career for nearly two-and-a-half years to raise her child."I believe motherhood is an important responsibility, but becoming an 'expert mother' and pursuing a career are not mutually exclusive. Women should have the freedom to choose their own path. With family support, workplace flexibility and good childcare, many women successfully balance both motherhood and demanding careers," she said.She says motherhood and professional success should never be presented as competing goals."Moreover, motherhood is a beautiful journey that makes a woman feel complete in its own way, while becoming an IAS officer is undoubtedly a great achievement. A woman's worth should never be measured by choosing one over the other; she should be empowered to excel in both if she wishes," she said.The survey also shows that women's experiences differ sharply depending on where they live, suggesting structural differences rather than a uniform cultural mindset.In Howrah, 83% of non-working women say household and childcare duties keep them out of paid work. Surat follows at 81%, with Pimpri Chinchwad, Bhopal and Dhanbad not far behind.By contrast, the figure falls to 38% in Coimbatore and 41% in Agra, while Hyderabad, Visakhapatnam, Srinagar and Kota also report substantially lower numbers.The wide variation suggests that women’s participation in paid work may be influenced not only by social attitudes but also by factors such as childcare facilities, transport, workplace flexibility and local employment opportunities.The contrast between cities underlines a larger reality: childcare remains largely a private family responsibility rather than public infrastructure.India has dramatically expanded girls' access to education over the past two decades. But the systems that allow women to remain in paid employment—affordable crèches, reliable daycare centres, after-school care, safe transport and flexible workplaces—have not expanded at the same pace.For many mothers, every working day begins with the same question: who will look after the children?The experience of Kanchan Jha, a mathematics teacher at a private school in Surat, reflects that reality. She became a mother at 29 and again at 33, spending 12 years away from paid work to care for her children before returning to teaching.Asked whether she had considered leaving her job because of childcare, she says:"Like many working mothers, there have been moments when balancing work and childcare felt challenging. However, with support from family and proper planning, it is possible to continue pursuing both personal and professional goals."She recalls that the toughest phase wasn't teaching itself, but trying to do everything at once."The biggest challenge was managing time and dealing with the feeling that neither role was getting my complete attention. Balancing school responsibilities, household work, and the emotional needs of a young child required careful planning and a strong support system."Her answer to what would help more women stay employed echoes what the data suggests."Affordable and reliable childcare support would make the biggest difference. Access to quality childcare would help many mothers continue their careers while ensuring that their children receive proper care and attention."The gaps in this support system are also visible in the implementation of existing policies.The Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act, 2017 mandates crèche facilities in many establishments, while MGNREGA also provides for childcare at worksites. But more than 85% of India's working women are employed in the informal sector, placing them outside the maternity law's coverage. Even among establishments where crèches are mandatory, compliance remains poor. A 2019 VV Giri National Labour Institute study found that three out of four organisations with over 50 employees lacked the required childcare facilities.The challenge is particularly acute in agriculture, which employs more than 70% of working women, but where fewer than 1% receive maternity benefits. Under MGNREGA, awareness of childcare provisions remains low and their availability even lower. A survey in Rajasthan found that only 53% of women knew they were entitled to childcare facilities, while just 0.7% reported having a crèche at their worksite.Trust is another concern. Allegations of abuse at a Bengaluru daycare centre have also heightened concerns about children's safety, making many mothers even more hesitant to return to work.Women who remain in the workforce also face unequal outcomes.Across the 46 cities, salaried men earn an average of Rs 30,700 a month, compared with Rs 23,700 for women—a gap of about 23%, the NSO survey says.The disparity is wider in cities such as Kalyan-Dombivli, Navi Mumbai and Nagpur, where women earn close to half as much as men. Self-employed women fare even worse, earning less than half as much as their male counterparts.The data, however, does not suggest that women are working fewer hours. Employees across these cities work an average of nearly 50 hours a week, with salaried women in Rajkot and Faridabad among those recording the longest working hours.This is not only a question of gender equality. It is also an economic issue.India has seen a gradual rise in women's participation in the labour force. According to the latest Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS), the female labour force participation rate (LFPR) for those aged 15 and above rose to 35.3% in December 2025, up from 35.1% in November. Rural female LFPR reached 40.1%, while the urban rate stood at 25.3%, highlighting the persistent gap between urban and rural India.At the same time, the NSO survey of 46 cities shows that 69% of non-working urban women cite childcare and household responsibilities as the primary reason for staying out of the workforce. Together, the two datasets suggest that while more women are entering the labour market, caregiving responsibilities continue to prevent many—particularly in urban India—from participating in paid employment.The challenge, therefore, is not simply creating jobs. It is creating the conditions that enable women to take them. Without affordable childcare, flexible workplaces and better support systems, many mothers will continue to face the same practical question each morning: who will look after the children?The survey identifies caregiving as the most commonly cited reason non-working women remain outside the labour force. Nearly seven in ten women reported it as a barrier, although the figure varied from 38% in Coimbatore to 83% in Howrah.Nibha Singh Mahar and Kanchan Jha both returned to work after taking career breaks to raise their children. They cited family support, planning, workplace flexibility and access to reliable childcare as important factors in balancing the two roles.Kanchan described affordable and reliable childcare as the measure that would make the biggest difference for working mothers.Nibha, meanwhile, said motherhood and professional success should not be treated as competing choices.“A woman’s worth should never be measured by choosing one over the other; she should be empowered to excel in both if she wishes,” she said.
Article Summary: National Statistics Office survey covering India’s 46 largest urban centres found that childcare and household responsibilities keep 69% of non-working women out of the labour force. For many women, unpaid care work remains a greater barrier to employment than education or qualifications. More than 60% of women graduates are neither working nor looking for work.

Article Title: Rajya Sabha MPs' salaries, perks cost exchequer Rs 262 crore in two years
Article Text:
Rajya Sabha members are entitled to benefits under 21 different expenditure heads

NEW DELHI: Over Rs 262 crore spent on Rajya Sabha MPs' salaries, allowances and other benefits during the last two financial years, with more than Rs 151 crore going towards allowances and travel alone, according to a reply obtained under the Right to Information (RTI) Act.The RTI response showed that expenditure on sitting Rajya Sabha members rose sharply from more than Rs 98 crore in 2024-25 to over Rs 163 crore in 2025-26, marking an increase of nearly Rs 65 crore in a year.The expenditure covered salaries, domestic and international travel, office expenses, medical reimbursements and other facilities provided to MPs.In 2024-25, the government spent over Rs 25.71 crore on salaries, Rs 8.2 crore on medical bills, Rs 24.99 crore on travel expenses and more than Rs 33.33 crore on allowances.In 2025-26, salaries increased to over Rs 44.6 crore, while medical bills stood at Rs 9.6 crore. Travel expenses rose to Rs 36 crore, and allowances exceeded Rs 58.78 crore, according to the reply provided by the Rajya Sabha Secretariat in response to an RTI application filed by Chander Shekhar Gaur.Rajya Sabha members are entitled to benefits under 21 different expenditure heads, including salaries, allowances, domestic and foreign travel, office expenses and medical reimbursements.Other expenditure heads include printing and publication, digital equipment, fuel and lubricants, professional services, repair and maintenance, subsidies and other revenue-related expenses.
Article Summary: Over Rs 262 crore spent on Rajya Sabha MPs' salaries, allowances and other benefits. More than Rs 151 crore went towards allowances and travel alone, according to a reply obtained under the Right to Information (RTI) Act. The expenditure covered salaries, domestic and international travel, office expenses, medical reimbursements and other facilities.

Article Title: 'In favour of the Women's Reservation Bill but...': DMK presses Centre on delimitation
Article Text:
AIDWA also urge immediate implementation

Chirag Paswan backs delimitation

NEW DELHI: The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam ( DMK ) on Sunday reiterated its support for 33 per cent reservation for women in the Lok Sabha, but urged the Centre to implement the quota based on the current strength of the house and provide greater clarity on the proposed delimitation exercise.The issue was raised by DMK MP Tiruchi Siva during the all-party meeting convened by the Centre ahead of the Monsoon Session of Parliament, which begins on Monday.Speaking to reporters after the all-party meeting, Siva said the party supported the Women's Reservation Bill but remained concerned about its linkage with delimitation."The DMK is in favour of the Women's Reservation Bill on the current strength in the Lok Sabha, but we want more clarity on the delimitation issue," Siva said.The senior DMK leader also cautioned that the delimitation exercise should not disadvantage southern states, which have recorded lower population growth over the years."If it affects the southern states, it should be put in abeyance for 25 years," Siva said.The Women's Reservation Bill, officially known as the Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam, was passed by Parliament in September 2023. It provides for 33 per cent reservation for women in the Lok Sabha, state legislative assemblies and the Delhi legislative assembly.However, its implementation has been linked to the first delimitation exercise after the next Census.The Centre had introduced the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill in April this year to expedite the delimitation process and enable implementation of the reservation before the 2029 Lok Sabha elections. However, the constitutional amendment failed to secure the required two-thirds majority in the Lok Sabha during the Budget Session. It received 298 votes in favour and 230 against.Meanwhile, the CPI(M)-affiliated All India Democratic Women's Association (AIDWA) has also stepped up pressure for the immediate implementation of the legislation without waiting for the Census or delimitation exercise.AIDWA Himachal Pradesh state secretary Pharma Chauhan said the organisation had urged the Congress and other opposition parties to back implementation of the law in its original form during the Monsoon Session."The Census and delimitation conditions should be removed. The legislation should be implemented without any further delay," Chauhan told ANI.She argued that despite Parliament passing the legislation nearly three years ago, women were yet to benefit from the constitutional guarantee."It is unfortunate that despite the passage of the legislation, it has not been implemented. Today, only around 70 women represent nearly half the country's population in Parliament. If 33 per cent reservation were implemented, about 183 women would be elected to the 543-member Lok Sabha," she said.Responding to the demand, Himachal Pradesh Congress Committee president Vinay Kumar said that the party will forward the demand to its national leadership."The Congress has always stood with women and will continue to support them.
